notes.anchor: this letter is in my secretary's hand
notes.note: Dictation to a librarius (secretary) rather than autograph writing was a mark of how pressed Cicero was; an autograph letter signalled intimacy or confidentiality.
Cicero's dialogue De Oratore, completed in 55 BC; he cites his own practice in it as a precedent and defers tagging it here as a self-reference.
The De Republica, the dialogue Cicero is composing in 54 BC, set dramatically in 129 BC; a self-reference to a work in progress.
P. Cornelius Scipio Aemilianus Africanus, the conqueror of Carthage (146) and Numantia (133); the principal speaker of the De Republica, whose 'Dream of Scipio' closes the work.
L. Furius Philus, consul 136 BC, a learned member of the Scipionic circle; in the De Republica he is assigned the case against justice in book 3.
C. Laelius Sapiens, consul 140 BC, the intimate friend of Scipio Aemilianus and a principal interlocutor of the De Republica; also the title figure of Cicero's De Amicitia.
M'. Manilius, consul 149 BC and a noted jurist; one of the older speakers of the De Republica.
Q. Aelius Tubero, a Stoic and nephew of Scipio Aemilianus; one of the younger participants in the dialogue.
P. Rutilius Rufus, consul 105 BC, a Stoic statesman later condemned and exiled in a notorious miscarriage of justice; Cicero presents him as the source for the conversation.
Q. Mucius Scaevola the augur and C. Fannius, both of whom married daughters of Laelius; the kinship explains their presence in the dialogue.
C. Fannius, consul 122 BC, son-in-law of Laelius and a historian; one of the younger speakers of the De Republica.

notes.anchor: the way Aristotle does in the works he calls exoteric
notes.note: Aristotle's 'exoteric' (popular, published) works, as opposed to his esoteric school treatises, were prized in antiquity for their literary prefaces; Cicero models his own book-proems on them.
Q. Mucius Scaevola the augur, jurist and son-in-law of Laelius; Atticus complained that Cicero withdrew him after book 1, and Cicero here defends the dramaturgical choice.

notes.anchor: our divine Plato did in his \textit{Republic}
notes.note: Plato's Republic (Politeia), the dialogue Cicero takes as the structural model for his own De Republica; he cites its opening to justify easing Scaevola out of the conversation.

notes.anchor: When Socrates had come down to Cephalus
notes.note: The Republic opens with Socrates visiting the elderly, wealthy Cephalus at his house in the Piraeus; after a short exchange Cephalus leaves for a sacrifice and is not heard from again.
The harbour-town of Athens, the dramatic setting of Plato's Republic.
The country estate at Tusculum belonging to L. Licinius Crassus (cos. 95), the dramatic setting of much of the De Oratore; here Cicero gives the De Republica a similar villa setting.

notes.anchor: the remaining books carry technical exposition
notes.note: Greek technologia: the systematic doctrinal core of the later books, which Cicero felt unsuited to the genial old Scaevola who suited only the opening conversation.
Pilia, the wife of Atticus, whose affairs Cicero is handling as a favour to his friend; he notes it will also commend him to his own daughter Tullia.

A law of 62 BC requiring that the text of a bill be deposited in the treasury before promulgation; here it is the statute under which C. Cato faced (and beat) prosecution for his irregular tribunate of 56 BC.
The lex Aelia et Fufia, mid-second-century legislation regulating obstruction of public business by religious observance (obnuntiatio); a second count against C. Cato.

notes.anchor: the fifth day before the Nones of Quintilis
notes.note: 3 July by the inclusive Roman count. Quintilis was the old name for the month later renamed July in honour of Julius Caesar.

notes.anchor: The senatorial decree which these consuls have passed about the provinces
notes.note: A decree of the consuls of 54 (Ap. Claudius Pulcher and L. Domitius Ahenobarbus) on the assignment of consular provinces; the 'whosoever hereafter' formula struck Cicero as unlikely to stand.
M. Aemilius Scaurus's aedileship of 58 BC was remembered for games of extraordinary lavishness; here it is an electoral asset as he stands for the consulship.
Scaurus's father was M. Aemilius Scaurus, the long-serving princeps senatus (cos. 115); his prestige carried weight with the rural voters.
Cisalpine Gaul, where Pompey's veteran settlers and Caesar's soldiers formed a bloc of votes that Memmius hoped to draw on.
Me hercule, a common Roman asseveration invoking Hercules; a mild oath of emphasis rather than a religious appeal.
